I wrote a small [Hypotheis](http://hypothesis.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) test case for `seek` with `SEEK_SET`:

```python
import pytest
import io

from boltons import ioutils
from hypothesis import strategies as st, given


def make_spooled_stringio():
    return ioutils.SpooledStringIO(32)


@given(text=st.text(),
       data=st.data())
@pytest.mark.parametrize('make_sio', [
    io.StringIO,
    make_spooled_stringio,
])
def test_seek(text, data, make_sio):
    where = data.draw(st.integers(min_value=0, max_value=len(text)))
    s = make_sio()
    s.write(text)
    s.seek(where)
    assert s.read() == text[where:]
```

The invariant under test is that seeking forward from the beginning any amount between 0 and the length of the string and then reading the remainder should return a string equal to the slice beginning there.

All Hypothesis test data passes with `io.StringIO`, but it fails with `SpooledStringIO` and the following inputs:

```python
sio = SpooledStringIO(32)
sio.write(u'\x8000')
sio.seek(2)
sio.read()  # prints u'', not u'0'
```

This appears to be unexpected behavior courtesy of `EncodedFile`

```
>>> c = EncodedFile(io.BytesIO(), data_encoding='utf-8')
>>> c.write(u'\x8000'.encode('utf-8'))
>>> c.seek(0)
0L
>>> len(c.read(2).decode('utf-8'))
3
````

That is, a request to read 2 code points actually returns 3!

It's certainly true that *your* code is on point!  But given the difficulty presented by `EncodingFile`, It occurs to me that it may be worthwhile to take a different approach.  What if you used `utf-32` instead of `utf-8`?  Because UTF-32 is a fixed-width encoding, you wouldn't need `EncodedFile`, but instead could just do modulo 4 arithmetic for seeks and tells.  It's worth noting that CPython itself uses one of several fixed-width representations of Unicode strings to avoid the hairy code you've successfully written and also for the sake of greater efficiency; with a fixed width encoding you can seek backwards from the end without having to go forward from the beginning!

`apk version -l "<"`:

```
['wayland-1.13.0-r0                       < 1.14.0-r2']
```

I was able to fix it like this:

```
package_old, package_new = [x.strip() for x in line.split('<')]
new_version, new_release = package_new.rsplit('-r')
pkg_string, old_release = package_old.rsplit('-r')
name, old_version = pkg_string.rsplit('-', 1)
```

The summary record, and all DAF records, are 128 words, i.e. fWords(1:128) in FORTRAN, and pWords[0:128] in Python.

Assuming for the moment that begin and end are in the range 1 to 128 inclusive, and dropping the .value suffix for readability:

This call loads the [begin.value-1:end.value] (Python notation) window from the record starting at the address (data+0) (C notation - passed to dafgsr_c, it  does *not* load data starting at (data+begin-1) (C notation).  So in all cases, data[begin-1:whatever] (Python notation) is wrong.

I picked 125 because the summary record uses 3 words at the beginning for the linked list and single summary count, I realized I should have made it 128 to ensure the vector was big enough to avoid a segfault in all cases.

The better thing to do is to initially size the data vector appropriately i.e.

    data = stypes.emptyDoubleVector(1 + min(1+[end,128]) - max([begin,1]))

Then there is no reason to truncate the data on return.  I will change this wrapper to do that.

Although an argument could be made that whatever the user passes implies that they expect a vector of lenght (1+end-begin); if I was doing this in C, I would provide data as a pointer to that much memory available..

If begin >= end, then I suspect stypes.emptyDoubleVector, or something it calls, will throw an exception, which is fine as it is a user error, so no need to check that here.

The way these async celery tasks work, is that one part of Treeherder schedules a named task, it then gets added to the queue on the Rabbitmq server, and then a worker later fetches the task from the queue and tries to match the name of the task against those defined. In practice that means that a race condition can occur, whereby a task gets queued up with the old name, but doesn't get run before the deployment of this PR completes, and so the worker then receives a task that doesn't match any known task, since they now have the new names.

As such I think we'll need to keep both the old and new task name here for now, so there aren't tasks silently dropped when this is deployed. eg: 
* Add the new task name alongside the old task name
* Make the parts of Treeherder that schedule the task use the new name
* Deploy those changes
* Later on in another deployment, remove the old task name since the queues will now definitely be empty.

This `json.dumps` come from `flask` namespace (http://flask.pocoo.org/docs/0.10/api/#flask.json.dumps) which changes the behaviour to use as encoder the class referenced by the flask app attribute `json_encoder`. That is the expected behaviour while using JSON encoders with flask. So you can personalize the encoder changing the `app.json_encoder`.

References:
http://flask.pocoo.org/snippets/119/

this API (unnecessarily) couples two things:
1. whether the *current active span* should be consulted as an implicit parent, and
2. whether the *span we are starting* should be made active

`start_active_span` is supposed to be about the latter thing... IMO, both `start_active_span` *and* `start_manual_span` should consult the current active span and use it as a parent if no references were provided.

I.e., this function signature should have an optional `references=` param.

I'd love to do that but some of us have been pushing for this for a year+ at this point and haven't been able to get approval. There's been a lot of renewed interest and frustration recently so it's possible things will change this time, but I'm not super confident this will happen in the short term. I'm a little hesitant to block this PR on those decisions, but if you want I can bring them up in the next week or two and see what we can accomplish.

What I'd like to do is reach out the Lexicon package maintainer, explain our situation and what we'd like to do, and see how receptive they are to the changes we'd like to make. This can help inform what we do here. I think it'd be good if you talked to them since you're the most familiar with the library but I can reach out first if you prefer.

Also, while I understand the inefficiency of the guess-and-check method of determining a DNS zone to modify with Lexicon, what are the other downsides to this approach? Is the code significantly more complex or error prone? How does doing this negatively impact the user? I just want to make sure I properly understand the problems here so we can make the right decision.

I talked about this with Erica for a bit and I think we should have a design meeting about packaging these plugins this week. My inclination right now is to put this plugin as an external package if we can reasonably move it into core Certbot if we want later without causing problems for users. I think this is doable but I want to talk and think about it to make sure. Please let me know what you think!
